title: Ring-3 cache replays expired or lower-sequence signed revocation snapshots as fresh

affected_locations.label: root_control
affected_locations.detail: Snapshots carry signed monotonic sequence and the dedicated store rejects non-increasing candidates.

affected_locations.label: source_and_broken_control
affected_locations.detail: Refresh verifies only authenticity, then unconditionally replaces held state and stamps caller-supplied now as the new freshness instant; no sequence or expires_at check.

affected_locations.label: sink
affected_locations.detail: Decision treats the replayed snapshot as current, validates tokens against its reduced revocation set, and allows routes up to the Connected ceiling.

source.attacker_control: compromised, lagging, or replayable distribution channel can resend an authentic prior snapshot

closest_control.why_incomplete: A valid old signature proves origin, not freshness or monotonic advance; refresh bypasses MonotonicRevocationStore and never checks the signed expiry.

sink_broken_control.detail: old state is installed, marked reachable/fresh, and used for authorization and cloud-route decisions

impact: A previously revoked token/key/tenant can become valid offline again and a stale snapshot can reopen CloudAllowed routing, defeating offline fail-closed behavior.
plausibility: High at the library boundary; runtime reachability is deferred because this snapshot contains no non-test Ring3Cache integrator.

candidate_local_validation.evidence: fabric-revocation provides the exact sequence control at lines 241-259, while wsf-cache refresh at lines 83-93 does not use it or parse expires_at; decide uses only local refresh age.

attack_path_facts: Attacker needs an authentic older snapshot, not the signing key.
Refresh accepts it even if sequence is lower or expires_at is in the past.
Refresh resets last_refresh_secs and reachable, so connectivity can become Connected.
Token validation consults only the replayed revocation contents and does not check snapshot expiry.

title: Revoked tokens remain authorized to unseal regulated payloads in the WSF API composition

affected_locations.label: root_control
affected_locations.detail: SealService supports a fail-closed revocation store, but the store is optional and absence returns Ok.

affected_locations.label: production_composition
affected_locations.detail: The network WSF API constructs SealService::new and never calls with_revocation_store.

affected_locations.label: sink
affected_locations.detail: After basic signature/expiry checks, unseal reaches Transit decrypt and returns plaintext.

source.attacker_control: holder of a previously valid token continues presenting it after out-of-band revocation

closest_control.control: verify_token checks signed on-token status, expiry, and optional current snapshot
closest_control.why_incomplete: revocation_status is immutable signed token state; later revocations require the external store, but None is allowed and returns success.

sink_broken_control.detail: OpenBao unwrap and authenticated envelope open return regulated plaintext

impact: A deprovisioned subject, revoked tenant, or compromised signing-key token can continue decrypting envelopes until token expiry.
plausibility: High: the shipped WSF API binary wires the vulnerable default and exposes the unseal route behind transport authentication, which a revoked token holder may still possess for its tenant.

candidate_local_validation.evidence: SealService::new sets revocation=None at lines 195-202; check_revocation returns Ok at 291-293; wsf-api main lines 185-192 uses new without the fail-closed builder; unseal returns plaintext at 435-442.

attack_path_facts: Attacker retains a validly signed, unexpired token after revocation publication.
WSF transport principal and token tenant checks do not consult revocation.
Default SealService has no store, so check_revocation allows.
Unseal decrypts the tenant data key and returns plaintext.

title: Revoked tokens remain authorized to seal data and mint trusted provenance in the WSF API composition

affected_locations.label: root_control
affected_locations.detail: Optional revocation wiring is fail-closed only when explicitly enabled; absence is accepted.

affected_locations.label: production_composition
affected_locations.detail: The network WSF API creates SealService without a revocation store.

affected_locations.label: sink
affected_locations.detail: Seal uses OpenBao Transit and service signer to return a trusted envelope.

source.attacker_control: holder of a token revoked after issuance

closest_control.control: basic token signature/expiry and optional snapshot check
closest_control.why_incomplete: the current revocation control is disabled by default and the production composition does not enable it.

sink_broken_control.detail: custody service wraps a key and provenance signer attests an envelope for the revoked identity

impact: A revoked identity can continue consuming custody/signing authority and create envelopes whose trusted thread attributes authorization to the revoked token.
plausibility: High at the exposed WSF API composition.

candidate_local_validation.evidence: Same default-allow revocation path as RWC-002, but independently reachable seal operation proceeds to Transit encryption and service signing.

attack_path_facts: Attacker retains an unexpired token after revocation.
The production WSF API SealService has revocation=None.
check_revocation returns Ok.
Seal obtains OpenBao custody service and service-signing operations.

title: Any sufficiently cleared token in a tenant can unseal another subject's owner-bound envelope

affected_locations.label: binding_source
affected_locations.detail: Seal binds owner_subject_hash from the authorizing token into authenticated envelope context.

affected_locations.label: broken_authorization
affected_locations.detail: Unseal checks envelope version, tenant, audience, classification, and label operation but never compares owner_subject_hash to the presenting token subject_hash.

affected_locations.label: wrapper_control
affected_locations.detail: The REST wrapper checks only WSF principal audience and tenant against the token before delegating to unseal.

affected_locations.label: sink
affected_locations.detail: The service decrypts under the shared tenant Transit key and returns plaintext.

source.attacker_control: authenticated same-tenant subject supplies its own valid token and a victim subject's envelope

closest_control.why_incomplete: The envelope explicitly declares an owner, but authorization stops at tenant scope; the per-tenant key also cannot distinguish subjects.

sink_broken_control.detail: tenant-wide Transit unwrap and open_envelope recover victim plaintext

impact: Cross-subject disclosure of regulated payloads within a tenant, defeating the owner binding that is AEAD- and signature-protected.
plausibility: High: both direct wsf-seal HTTP and wsf-api accept caller-supplied token plus envelope; no subject comparison exists in either path.

candidate_local_validation.evidence: For two tokens with equal tenant and adequate classification but different subject_hash, all conditions at 389-422 evaluate allow; transit_key_for uses only tenant at 428, while owner_subject_hash is never read in unseal.

attack_path_facts: Attacker is an authenticated subject in the same tenant.
Victim envelope binding contains victim subject_hash, but this is not compared.
Attacker token satisfies tenant, audience, classification, and label operation checks.
Tenant-scoped Transit key unwraps the envelope key and plaintext is returned.

title: Nested attenuation changes the spend key and resets a capability's budget

affected_locations.label: root_design_gap
affected_locations.detail: lineage_key explicitly keys only one sibling level by immediate parent and documents that full-subtree accounting needs external lineage.

affected_locations.label: attenuation_control
affected_locations.detail: Authenticated attenuation sets child.parent_id to the immediate parent and resets child spent counters after comparing only embedded parent remaining.

affected_locations.label: reachable_wrapper
affected_locations.detail: Authenticated REST attenuation passes max_child_depth=None, so a caller can repeatedly attenuate descendants.

affected_locations.label: authorization_sink
affected_locations.detail: Gateway budget preflight and accounting use lineage_key, so each new immediate-parent id creates a fresh ledger namespace.

instance_key: wsf-api recursive attenuation -> aog-gateway lineage metering

source.attacker_control: authorized token holder selects new_token_id and repeats attenuation

closest_control.control: child caps cannot exceed the immediate parent's embedded remaining budget and spent fields reset
closest_control.why_incomplete: Runtime spend lives in a separate ledger and is not folded before attenuation; descendants use a different immediate-parent key, so prior lineage spend is invisible.

sink_broken_control.detail: budget gate and record_spend operate on the fresh lineage key, allowing another full cap

impact: A caller can exceed token, USD, and tool-call ceilings arbitrarily by spending, attenuating to a descendant, and repeating; tool-call budgets may gate privileged agent actions.
plausibility: High: the attenuation endpoint authenticates each descendant but permits unlimited depth, and the gateway uses the flawed immediate-parent key exactly as documented.

candidate_local_validation.evidence: Root R(cap=100) -> child C(parent=R, cap=100) spends under key R; C -> grandchild G(parent=C, cap=100) is accepted because C's embedded spent=0; G spends under fresh key C. Repeat at arbitrary depth because max_child_depth=None.

attack_path_facts: Attacker has any attenuable token with a budget.
Each child is genuinely signed, so signature checks do not stop the chain.
External gateway spend is not written back into the signed parent token.
Immediate-parent lineage keys differ at every depth.
The REST endpoint supplies no depth limit.
